author: cdedreuille
pr_title: feat: Introduce ToastApi for rich notifications in the new frontend system

author: freben
pr_title: cli: add CLI module plugin API and auto-discovery
text:
I think my question is, "is `require('@backstage/cli/config/...` itself as a pattern a problem, and will people ever want or need to dynamically replace those things".

Like, replacement of the jest transform. But at that point - as long as the reference to it is "visible" and under your own control - as is the case inside an .eslintrc - then the method of "dynamic" replacement is static instead - you just change the .eslintrc to point to your file.

But there are some such contracts that get case specific. To replace ts parameters you change tsconfig for example. So there's a level of indirection happening and it varies depending on which config thing we're talking about. I just recall some support questions asking "how do I tweak X" and it turns out it's tricky because you have to entirely break glass and leave our tooling to do it.

What if we never invented the overrides parameter to the jest transform, and instead had a "module plugin pattern" of sorts that _injects `/config/` layer behaviors_? As a separate initiative to the commands

author: Copilot
pr_title: cli: migrate remaining commands from commander to cleye

author: cdedreuille
pr_title: feat(ui): introduce semantic color token families and deprecate legacy tokens
text: For added context we decided to roll back the changes on the neutral family. This removed the need for this PR to become a breaking change and reduce migration complexity at this stage.

author: pav-kv
pr_title: storage: add MultiEngineCompactionScheduler
text: Maybe wait for `!isGranting` in `Register` too, so that we never have this slice mutated during this loop? It may be fine that it is, but simplicity seems preferable over optimizing for one-off startup/shutdown cases.

author: msbutler
pr_title: backup: add support for locality aware backup compaction
text: nit: by convention, once a instance id matches a locality, we break out of the locality inner loop. example: https://github.com/andrew-r-thomas/cockroach/blob/compaction-loc-aware/pkg/sql/distsql_physical_planner.go#L1361

author: Copilot
pr_title: backup: add ListRestorableBackup helper
text:
The documentation states "we always pick the backup with the oldest start time among those with the same end time," but this contradicts the actual implementation. The code keeps the last index encountered (line 266), which, given the ascending start time sort order for tie-breaking (line 312), means it keeps the backup with the newest (largest) start time, not the oldest. This is the intended behavior to preserve non-compacted backups over compacted ones. The documentation should be corrected to state "newest start time" instead of "oldest start time".
```suggestion
// with the newest start time among those with the same end time. Also note that
```

author: DarrylWong
pr_title: mixedversion: add same series upgrades
text:
One thing to note is that for `separateProcessDeployment`, we always use `latestPredecessor`. The reason being that serverless usually runs on the latest patch, so if we hit a bug on an older patch, it either:

1. Exists on the latest patch, we could've caught it anyway
2. Does not exist on the latest patch, no action required since nothing is running this version anymore

At the same time, I think this makes it a prime candidate for stressing same series upgrades since it means we run a ton of them. But only for the newest release branch, since a bug hit on an older release branch has the same reasoning as above?

Not something I think needs to be handled in the PR, just thinking out loud.

author: dt
pr_title: backup: add architecture reference docs
text: NB: revision history backups have historically been more prone to bugs as they are less tested and also since the widely used "span" for describing key ranges is typically in terms of only the user key, not its time-stamp suffixed engine key encoding so e.g. mid-row resume spans were at one point mis-represented in metadata. Their SSTs are also incompatible with fast/online restore. Future work in this area may choose to deprecate these and replace them with point-in-time normal inc backups accompanied by some sort of "revision log" that could be replayed by restore to serve the same point-in-time-recovery use-case, with lower RPO as well (since revision history backups are still periodic backups that can't reliably be run more often than every X minutes at most).

author: dt
pr_title: backup: add architecture reference docs
text: (claude) Added a paragraph covering all of this: less testing, the `Span` type historically being just user-key without an MVCC suffix (leading to mid-row resume-span bugs), fast/online incompatibility, and possible future deprecation in favor of point-in-time backups plus a revision log that restore replays — same use case, lower RPO.

author: jeffswenson
pr_title: crosscluster/producer: transactional buffer prototype with temp storage
text: We should have a randomized test that builds a frontier out of the checkpoints emitted by a subscription and asserts that we never see data come out of the ordered feed that is behind the checkpoint.

author: jeffswenson
pr_title: crosscluster/producer: transactional buffer prototype with temp storage
text: This test should create a frontier from the checkpoint and assert that we never see an event that is older than the timestamp tracked for its span in the checkpoint's frontier.

author: pav-kv
pr_title: kvstorage: add raft log replay for WAG catch-up
text:
Should this be an empty timestamp rather than `MaxTimestamp`? These are range-local, not versioned keys (don't confuse with ones that live in meta2). Also not sure we need `Inconsistent: true`.

Not sure why multiple other places read this key this way, e.g.
https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach/blob/725a6009f2ace3de97d595db0242878726c53053/pkg/kv/kvserver/replica.go#L2541-L2549

I thought we never write an intent to this key, but I could be wrong, need to check.

author: sanchit-CRL
pr_title: rpc: add SAN-based authentication for root and node users
text: For SAN enabled we never fallback to anything else apart from Subject matching. If subject matching fails, we should fail

author: pav-kv
pr_title: kvstorage: add live WAG truncation
text:
As commented elsewhere, the `startIndex` reading can move inside the method.

The thing is: having the `startIndex` in the method signature tells us "it's fine to call with any start index". Whereas we always want to truncate a prefix. Calling it with anything other than start index would seem incorrect, because it maintains `lastTruncatedWAGIndex` whose invariant is that the entire prefix is truncated.

author: sumeerbhola
pr_title: sql,admission: support cpu reporting and admission

author: alyshanjahani-crl
pr_title: obs,sql: add statement fingerprint store
text:
The convention is to grow before adding.

If we add and the grow fails we risk underreporting on our mem monitor

Same pattern in appendPending

I think adhering to grow before add is fine in this case - if we fail - we retry adding to the cache the next time we see the statement - which is how StatementStore deals w/ persistence failure too.

author: jeffswenson
pr_title: logical: create ordered txnfeed
text:
This isn't just a test behavior. It's a stated requirement for an ordered feed. A requirement for the ordered subscription is that a run of data is always followed by a checkpoint. This behavior is required to avoid a commit latency bubble in the steady state that is caused when we peek past the last datum. If there was no trailing checkpoint that peak would stall.

Also: we never really drain these subscriptions in production. You can stop LDR but that would show up as cancellation.

author: fqazi
pr_title: schemafeed: reduce overhead of schema_locked
text:
Done.

I modified this to store the latest version observed, so that we never hold onto a stale lease. The heldInfo now tracks if the maximum version observed.

author: jade-guiton-dd
pr_title: [RFC] Batch processor migration
text: (Duplicating what I said on Slack for visibility) I agree that they need to migrate, the problem is that making the batch processor a no-op at the end of the migration period is just breaking the behavior further, instead of giving a clear signal that they need to upgrade because we no longer support the processor. To me, a clearer signal would be (for instance) an error message at startup because the processor no longer exists.

author: 57Ajay
pr_title: Introduce UserAgent field to allow overriding the default gRPC user-agent string.
text:
Currently, `otlpexporter` is the only component that calls `ClientConfig.ToClientConn`. For that component, UserAgent is fully wired and functional. For any other component that adopts `ClientConfig.ToClientConn` in the future, the field would be available in their config - but they would need to read it explicitly, the same way `otlpexporter` does today.
Regarding wiring it automatically inside getGrpcDialOptions in configgrpc so all future callers get it for free - that is definitely possible. The reason it currently lives in otlpexporter rather than configgrpc is that during an earlier review round, there was a concern from @dmathieu  about setting the user-agent twice (once in configgrpc and once via the extraOpts passed by otlpexporter). To avoid that, the wiring was kept only in otlpexporter.
If the preferred approach is to have it work universally via configgrpc, I am happy to restructure it.
